feedback_text:
DIAGNOSIS: 22 failing sub-attributes (35.7 pts recoverable), 1 passing. 5 regressions detected — recovery is top priority.

==================================================
REGRESSIONS (RECOVER THESE FIRST — highest priority):
==================================================

  REGRESSED: ttl_expiry_system — was 82% at iter 5, now 51% (-31%)
    What was lost: The actual implementation of the background eviction thread with JoinHandle, shutdown signaling, and graceful cleanup procedures. The best version (iteration 5) contained the complete thread lifecycle management code including thread spawning, shutdown channels, and proper cleanup, while the current version only has comments mentioning background eviction but no actual implementation.
    Why it dropped: The current iteration appears to have been truncated or incomplete, showing only the header comments, imports, configuration structs, and error types but missing the core cache implementation including the background thread management code that was present in iteration 5. This caused the background_thread_lifecycle score to drop from a higher value to 0.0 since no actual thread implementation is present.
    → RECOVER: Restore the complete cache implementation from iteration 5 that includes: (1) the background eviction thread spawning with JoinHandle storage, (2) shutdown signaling mechanism using channels or atomic flags, (3) graceful thread cleanup in the Drop implementation, and (4) the timer wheel or heap-based expiration queue structure that was contributing to the timer_structure_efficiency score. Ensure the full implementation is included, not just the header portions.

  REGRESSED: rust_api_guidelines_compliance — was 100% at iter 5, now 80% (-20%)
    What was lost: The LruTracker<K> struct is missing standard trait implementations that were present in the best version. While CacheEntry<V> implements Debug and Clone traits, LruTracker<K> only implements Debug, creating an inconsistency in trait coverage across the codebase.
    Why it dropped: During iteration 6, the LruTracker<K> struct lost its Clone trait implementation (and potentially other standard traits like PartialEq), while CacheEntry<V> retained its full trait implementations. This created an imbalance where some types have comprehensive trait coverage while others are missing expected standard traits.
    → RECOVER: Add Clone trait implementation to LruTracker<K> struct to match the trait coverage of CacheEntry<V>. Also ensure LruTracker implements other applicable standard traits like PartialEq where the generic type K supports them, using appropriate trait bounds like `#[derive(Debug, Clone)] pub struct LruTracker<K: Clone> { ... }` or conditional implementations.

  REGRESSED: lru_ordering_correctness — was 90% at iter 5, now 71% (-19%)
    What was lost: The best version (iteration 5) contained formal verification or comprehensive testing evidence for ordering invariants under concurrent load, which was completely removed in the current version. This included either mathematical proofs of invariant preservation or exhaustive test suites demonstrating 100% correctness of LRU ordering operations.
    Why it dropped: The current version removed all formal verification methodology and comprehensive testing evidence that demonstrated ordering correctness. While it maintains the intrusive doubly-linked list implementation, it no longer provides the rigorous proof or testing framework that validated the ordering invariants hold under all concurrent access patterns.
    → RECOVER: Add back the formal verification section or comprehensive testing methodology that proves ordering invariants are maintained under concurrent load. Include either: (1) mathematical proofs showing invariant preservation across all operations, or (2) exhaustive test suites with stress testing results demonstrating 100% correctness of LRU ordering under high concurrency, including edge cases like simultaneous access/eviction scenarios.

  REGRESSED: memory_ordering_correctness — was 91% at iter 5, now 82% (-9%)
    What was lost: The excerpts provided are nearly identical and cut off at the same point in the CacheError::Display implementation. Both versions show the same documentation, imports, CacheConfig, and error types. The actual implementation details that would demonstrate atomic ordering semantics, lock-free components, and ABA prevention mechanisms are not visible in either excerpt.
    Why it dropped: Cannot determine the cause of regression from the provided excerpts as they appear to be identical truncated versions showing only the module documentation, imports, configuration struct, and error enum. The critical implementation code containing atomic operations, memory ordering choices, lock-free algorithms, and ABA prevention mechanisms is missing from both excerpts.
    → RECOVER: Provide complete code excerpts that include the actual cache implementation with atomic operations, memory ordering specifications, lock-free read paths, and ABA prevention mechanisms to enable proper comparison and identification of what specific technical content was lost between iterations 5 and 6.

  REGRESSED: async_runtime_integration — was 35% at iter 5, now 26% (-9%)
    What was lost: The excerpts shown are identical between iterations 5 and 6, containing only the header documentation and basic structure. The regression must have occurred in implementation code that was present in iteration 5 but removed or degraded in iteration 6.
    Why it dropped: Based on the scorer's critique, iteration 6 removed or weakened async-compatible implementation that was present in iteration 5. The current version only provides std::sync primitives with documentation acknowledging async incompatibility, whereas iteration 5 likely had some level of async integration or tokio primitive usage that earned it a higher score.
    → RECOVER: Add back the async-compatible implementation that was present in iteration 5. This should include: 1) Implement a parallel async version using tokio::sync::RwLock instead of std::sync::RwLock, 2) Add async methods alongside the sync methods, 3) Implement basic cancellation handling using tokio::select! or CancellationToken, 4) Provide async-compatible background task management using tokio::spawn instead of std::thread, 5) Maintain the current documentation that acknowledges the distinction between sync and async versions.

DO NOT CHANGE (these sections are passing):
  ✓ deadlock_prevention_design: 100% — strong, lower priority

REQUIRED FIXES (ordered by point impact):

  FIX 1: ttl_expiry_system.ttl_timestamp_consistency (current: 75%, 2.4 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Implements sophisticated atomic TTL updates with proper CAS retry loops and detailed understanding of ARM architecture considerations.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if uses AtomicU64 timestamps with compare-and-swap updates, 0.7 if uses write locks consistently for TTL updates, 0.4 if uses read locks but potential races, 0.0 if unprotected TTL timestamp updates
    → ACTION: Consider adding validation that TTL updates maintain monotonic ordering to prevent time-travel scenarios.

  FIX 2: lru_ordering_correctness.ordering_invariants (current: 50%, 2.4 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Implements intrusive list with proper invariant maintenance but lacks formal verification or testing evidence for ordering correctness under concurrent load.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if invariants proven with formal verification or exhaustive testing showing 100% correctness, 0.7 if stress testing shows ≥99% correctness with identified edge cases, 0.4 if basic testing with some ordering violations under load, 0.0 if frequent ordering corruption
    Evidence found: Provides formal verification or testing evidence: not found in content; Beyond-rubric expertise on invariant preservation: no evidence of formal verification or comprehensive testing methodology
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Provides formal verification or testing evidence
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Beyond-rubric expertise on invariant preservation
      Evidence: no evidence of formal verification or comprehensive testing methodology
    → ACTION: Add stress testing results or formal verification showing that LRU ordering invariants are preserved under concurrent access patterns. Include test cases that verify ordering after concurrent promotions and evictions.

  FIX 3: ttl_expiry_system.background_thread_lifecycle (current: 25%, 2.1 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Code mentions background eviction thread in comments but does not implement the actual thread lifecycle management, shutdown signaling, or cleanup procedures.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if implements graceful shutdown with JoinHandle and bounded cleanup time, 0.7 if uses shutdown signal but unbounded wait, 0.4 if background thread but manual cleanup required, 0.0 if background thread without proper lifecycle
    Evidence found: Implements graceful shutdown with JoinHandle: not found in content; Uses shutdown signal mechanism: not found in content; Provides bounded cleanup time: not found in content; Beyond-rubric expertise on thread lifecycle: no evidence of background thread implementation
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Implements graceful shutdown with JoinHandle
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Uses shutdown signal mechanism
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Provides bounded cleanup time
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Beyond-rubric expertise on thread lifecycle
      Evidence: no evidence of background thread implementation
    → ACTION: Implement the background eviction thread with: `let handle = thread::spawn(move || { /* eviction loop */ }); let shutdown_signal = Arc<AtomicBool>::new(false);` and proper JoinHandle management for graceful shutdown.

  FIX 4: lru_ordering_correctness.temporal_complexity (current: 75%, 2.1 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Provides thorough O(1) complexity analysis with mathematical justification and comparison to alternative approaches.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if all operations proven O(1) amortized with detailed complexity analysis, 0.7 if O(1) average case with some O(log n) worst-case scenarios, 0.4 if O(n) operations present but acknowledged, 0.0 if O(n²) or worse
    → ACTION: Consider adding amortized analysis for epoch advancement and cleanup operations.

  FIX 5: memory_ordering_correctness.atomic_ordering_semantics (current: 75%, 2.1 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Demonstrates strong understanding of memory ordering with acquire-release semantics and detailed documentation of ARM-specific considerations for spurious CAS failures.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if uses acquire-release ordering consistently with documented reasoning for each choice, 0.7 if uses acquire-release but inconsistently, 0.4 if uses relaxed ordering with some barriers, 0.0 if uses only relaxed or no atomic operations
    → ACTION: Consider adding more specific ordering choices for different operation types (e.g., relaxed for pure counters vs acquire-release for state).
    ⚖ TRADEOFF: DO NOT sacrifice 'memory_ordering_correctness' for 'performance_optimization_analysis'. When these conflict, 'memory_ordering_correctness' wins unconditionally. Correctness of memory ordering and concurrency safety takes precedence over performance optimizations when they conflict, as safety violations can cause undefined behavior

  FIX 6: memory_ordering_correctness.aba_prevention (current: 75%, 2.1 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Implements comprehensive ABA prevention through epoch-based reclamation with generation tracking and cleanup queues.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if uses epoch-based reclamation or hazard pointers for memory management, 0.7 if uses generation counters or versioning, 0.4 if acknowledges ABA risk but incomplete mitigation, 0.0 if no ABA consideration in lock-free components
    → ACTION: Consider adding explicit documentation of how the epoch system prevents specific ABA scenarios in the intrusive list operations.
    ⚖ TRADEOFF: DO NOT sacrifice 'memory_ordering_correctness' for 'performance_optimization_analysis'. When these conflict, 'memory_ordering_correctness' wins unconditionally. Correctness of memory ordering and concurrency safety takes precedence over performance optimizations when they conflict, as safety violations can cause undefined behavior

  FIX 7: memory_bounds_enforcement.memory_bound_compliance (current: 25%, 2.0 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Defines memory bounds configuration and tracks entry sizes but lacks implementation of actual memory enforcement logic or testing evidence.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if memory usage never exceeds bounds + 5% buffer during stress testing, 0.7 if occasionally exceeds by up to 15%, 0.4 if frequent exceedance but eventual compliance, 0.0 if unbounded memory growth
    Evidence found: Provides stress testing evidence of bound compliance: not found in content; Handles concurrent memory pressure: not found in content; Beyond-rubric expertise on memory management: no evidence of advanced memory tracking or enforcement mechanisms
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Provides stress testing evidence of bound compliance
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Handles concurrent memory pressure
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Beyond-rubric expertise on memory management
      Evidence: no evidence of advanced memory tracking or enforcement mechanisms
    → ACTION: Implement memory enforcement: `if self.current_memory.load(Ordering::Acquire) + entry_size > self.config.max_memory_bytes { self.evict_lru_until_space(entry_size)?; }` with atomic memory tracking.

  FIX 8: concurrency_correctness_integration.lru_ttl_atomicity (current: 50%, 2.0 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Implements atomic TTL operations and epoch-based coordination but lacks explicit prevention of expired entry promotion in LRU operations.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if LRU promotions and TTL updates occur atomically preventing expired entries from being promoted, 0.7 if mostly coordinated with rare race conditions, 0.4 if some coordination but potential inconsistencies, 0.0 if LRU and TTL systems operate independently with race conditions
    Evidence found: Prevents expired entries from being promoted: not found in content; Beyond-rubric expertise on atomicity: no evidence of specific LRU-TTL coordination mechanisms
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Prevents expired entries from being promoted
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Beyond-rubric expertise on atomicity
      Evidence: no evidence of specific LRU-TTL coordination mechanisms
    → ACTION: Add TTL validation in LRU access: `fn access(&mut self, key: &K) { if let Some(entry) = self.get(key) { if !entry.is_expired() { self.lru_tracker.access(key); } } }`

  FIX 9: memory_bounds_enforcement.reference_cycle_prevention (current: 75%, 1.8 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Excellent cycle prevention through raw pointers and epoch-based reclamation, avoiding traditional Arc/Weak patterns entirely for better performance.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if uses Weak references for back-pointers and parent relationships with documented cycle analysis, 0.7 if mostly correct with minor potential cycles, 0.4 if some Arc cycles but manual cleanup, 0.0 if Arc cycles causing memory leaks
    → ACTION: Consider documenting the safety invariants more explicitly to help maintainers understand when raw pointer usage is safe.

  FIX 10: concurrency_correctness_integration.memory_ordering_consistency (current: 50%, 1.8 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Provides LRU-based eviction interface and memory safety through epochs but lacks implementation of actual memory-pressure eviction logic.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if eviction due to memory pressure respects LRU ordering and doesn't corrupt cache structure, 0.7 if mostly consistent with minor ordering issues, 0.4 if some integration but potential corruption, 0.0 if memory bounds and cache ordering conflict
    Evidence found: Eviction maintains cache invariants: not found in content; Beyond-rubric expertise on memory-ordering integration: no evidence of comprehensive integration testing
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Eviction maintains cache invariants
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Beyond-rubric expertise on memory-ordering integration
      Evidence: no evidence of comprehensive integration testing
    → ACTION: Implement memory-pressure eviction: `fn evict_for_memory(&mut self, needed_bytes: usize) -> Result<(), CacheError> { while self.memory_usage() + needed_bytes > self.max_memory { let lru_key = self.lru_tracker.least_recently_used()?; self.remove(lru_key)?; } }`

  FIX 11: async_runtime_integration.async_sync_primitives (current: 50%, 1.6 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Correctly identifies that std::sync primitives are not suitable for async contexts and documents this limitation, but doesn't provide async-compatible implementation.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if uses tokio::sync::RwLock and other async primitives correctly, 0.7 if mixes async and sync primitives appropriately, 0.4 if some async primitives but potential blocking, 0.0 if uses std sync primitives in async context
    Evidence found: Uses tokio::sync primitives for async operations: This implementation uses std::sync for blocking operations - not suitable for async contexts. Use tokio::sync::RwLock for async compatibility.; Provides async-aware synchronization: uses std::sync::RwLock throughout; Beyond-rubric expertise on async primitives: acknowledges async limitations but doesn't implement async solution
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Uses tokio::sync primitives for async operations
      Evidence: This implementation uses std::sync for blocking operations - not suitable for async contexts. Use tokio::sync::RwLock for async compatibility.
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Provides async-aware synchronization
      Evidence: uses std::sync::RwLock throughout
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Beyond-rubric expertise on async primitives
      Evidence: acknowledges async limitations but doesn't implement async solution
    → ACTION: Implement async variant using tokio::sync::RwLock: `pub struct AsyncLruCache<K, V> { data: Arc<tokio::sync::RwLock<HashMap<K, CacheEntry<V>>>> }` with .await on all lock acquisitions.

  FIX 12: performance_safety_tradeoff_analysis.safety_preservation_under_optimization (current: 75%, 1.6 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Demonstrates strong understanding of safety-performance tradeoffs through epoch-based reclamation and careful memory ordering, but lacks formal verification or comprehensive testing evidence.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if performance optimizations proven to maintain safety through formal analysis or exhaustive testing, 0.7 if extensive testing shows safety preservation, 0.4 if basic verification but some uncertainty, 0.0 if optimizations compromise safety guarantees
    Evidence found: Formal analysis or extensive testing of safety preservation: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Formal analysis or extensive testing of safety preservation
      Evidence: not found in content
    → ACTION: Add formal safety analysis or comprehensive stress testing results that prove safety properties are maintained under optimized lock-free operations.

  FIX 13: ttl_expiry_system.timer_structure_efficiency (current: 50%, 1.5 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Uses BTreeMap for time ordering and atomic timestamps for individual checks, but lacks a comprehensive timer wheel or heap-based expiration system for efficient bulk expiration.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if implements timer wheel or heap-based expiration queue, 0.7 if uses BTreeMap for time-ordered expiration, 0.4 if sorted vector with binary search, 0.0 if linear scan for expired entries
    Evidence found: Implements timer wheel or heap-based expiration: not found in content; Beyond-rubric expertise on timer structures: no evidence of advanced timer wheel or hierarchical timing structures
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Implements timer wheel or heap-based expiration
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Beyond-rubric expertise on timer structures
      Evidence: no evidence of advanced timer wheel or hierarchical timing structures
    → ACTION: Implement a timer wheel structure: `struct TimerWheel { buckets: Vec<Vec<(K, Instant)>>, current_bucket: AtomicUsize }` for O(1) expiration scheduling and efficient bulk cleanup.

  FIX 14: async_runtime_integration.cancellation_safety (current: 0%, 1.4 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: No cancellation safety mechanisms implemented. The sync-only design doesn't address async task cancellation scenarios.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if implements cancellation tokens with guaranteed cleanup and no resource leaks, 0.7 if handles cancellation but some potential resource issues, 0.4 if basic cancellation handling, 0.0 if no cancellation safety considerations
    Evidence found: Implements cancellation tokens: not found in content; Provides guaranteed cleanup on cancellation: not found in content; Handles task cancellation gracefully: not found in content; Beyond-rubric expertise on cancellation safety: no evidence of cancellation handling
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Implements cancellation tokens
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Provides guaranteed cleanup on cancellation
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Handles task cancellation gracefully
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Beyond-rubric expertise on cancellation safety
      Evidence: no evidence of cancellation handling
    → ACTION: For async variant, implement cancellation safety: `use tokio_util::sync::CancellationToken; async fn get_with_cancellation(&self, key: &K, token: CancellationToken) -> Result<Option<V>, Cancelled>`

  FIX 15: performance_safety_tradeoff_analysis.error_handling_optimization_compatibility (current: 75%, 1.4 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Excellent integration of error handling with performance optimizations, ensuring error recovery capabilities are preserved even in lock-free code paths.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if optimized paths maintain full error recovery capabilities with no degradation, 0.7 if mostly preserved with minor limitations, 0.4 if some error handling limitations in optimized paths, 0.0 if optimizations compromise error recovery
    → ACTION: Add specific testing of error recovery during lock-free operations to validate that the coordination system works correctly under concurrent optimization scenarios.

  FIX 16: memory_bounds_enforcement.long_term_stability (current: 50%, 1.2 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Implements cleanup mechanisms through epoch-based reclamation but lacks evidence of long-term stability testing or memory leak prevention validation.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if demonstrates stable memory usage over 24+ hours with <1MB/hour growth, 0.7 if stable over shorter periods with acceptable growth rate, 0.4 if some leaks but bounded, 0.0 if unbounded memory leaks
    Evidence found: Demonstrates stable memory usage over time: not found in content; Provides long-running stability evidence: not found in content; Beyond-rubric expertise on long-term stability: no evidence of long-term testing or stability analysis
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Demonstrates stable memory usage over time
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Provides long-running stability evidence
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Beyond-rubric expertise on long-term stability
      Evidence: no evidence of long-term testing or stability analysis
    → ACTION: Add long-term stability testing: run cache for 24+ hours with periodic memory usage reporting to demonstrate <1MB/hour growth and stable cleanup behavior.

  FIX 17: concurrency_correctness_integration.cross_system_invariants (current: 75%, 1.2 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Implements sophisticated cross-subsystem error coordination with generation tracking and atomic rollback, demonstrating advanced understanding of system-wide consistency.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if formal analysis or comprehensive testing shows all subsystems maintain consistency, 0.7 if extensive testing with high confidence in cross-system consistency, 0.4 if basic cross-system testing, 0.0 if subsystems can interfere with each other's invariants
    Evidence found: Formal analysis or comprehensive testing of cross-system consistency: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Formal analysis or comprehensive testing of cross-system consistency
      Evidence: not found in content
    → ACTION: Add formal verification or comprehensive integration tests that validate cross-system invariants under concurrent load and failure scenarios.

  FIX 18: error_handling_system_coherence.async_error_isolation (current: 0%, 1.2 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: No async error isolation mechanisms since the implementation uses std::sync and explicitly states it's not suitable for async contexts.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if async task panics or cancellations maintain cache invariants with proper cleanup, 0.7 if mostly isolated with minor potential issues, 0.4 if some isolation but potential cache corruption, 0.0 if async failures can corrupt cache state
    Evidence found: Async task panics maintain cache invariants: not found in content; Async task cancellations don't corrupt cache: not found in content; Async failures isolated from cache state: not found in content; Beyond-rubric expertise on async error isolation: no async implementation provided
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Async task panics maintain cache invariants
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Async task cancellations don't corrupt cache
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Async failures isolated from cache state
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Beyond-rubric expertise on async error isolation
      Evidence: no async implementation provided
    → ACTION: For async variant, implement panic isolation: `tokio::task::spawn(async move { /* background task */ }).await.unwrap_or_else(|e| { self.error_coordinator.handle_background_failure(e); });`

  FIX 19: async_runtime_integration.async_ecosystem_integration (current: 25%, 1.0 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Explicitly acknowledges async ecosystem incompatibility and provides guidance for async integration, but doesn't implement async-compatible version.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if follows tokio patterns and integrates with async ecosystem standards, 0.7 if good integration with minor deviations, 0.4 if basic async support but limited ecosystem integration, 0.0 if poor async ecosystem integration
    Evidence found: Follows tokio patterns and conventions: uses std::sync throughout; Integrates with async ecosystem standards: not suitable for async contexts; Provides async-compatible interfaces: blocking operations only
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Follows tokio patterns and conventions
      Evidence: uses std::sync throughout
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Integrates with async ecosystem standards
      Evidence: not suitable for async contexts
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Provides async-compatible interfaces
      Evidence: blocking operations only
    → ACTION: Create async-compatible version with proper ecosystem integration: implement `Stream` trait for cache events, use `tokio::time::interval` for background cleanup, and provide async-compatible builder pattern.

  FIX 20: performance_safety_tradeoff_analysis.tradeoff_documentation_quality (current: 50%, 1.0 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Provides good qualitative documentation of design tradeoffs and limitations but lacks quantitative analysis, benchmarks, or formal performance/safety tradeoff evaluation.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if provides quantitative analysis of safety/performance/maintainability tradeoffs with benchmarks and reasoning, 0.7 if good documentation with some quantitative support, 0.4 if basic tradeoff discussion, 0.0 if no tradeoff analysis or documentation
    Evidence found: Provides quantitative analysis of tradeoffs: not found in content; Includes benchmarks or performance analysis: not found in content; Beyond-rubric expertise on tradeoff analysis: good qualitative analysis but no quantitative benchmarks or formal tradeoff analysis
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Provides quantitative analysis of tradeoffs
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Includes benchmarks or performance analysis
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Beyond-rubric expertise on tradeoff analysis
      Evidence: good qualitative analysis but no quantitative benchmarks or formal tradeoff analysis
    → ACTION: Add quantitative tradeoff analysis: benchmark lock-free vs locked operations, measure memory overhead of epoch-based reclamation, and document specific performance/safety/maintainability tradeoffs with numbers.

  FIX 21: rust_api_guidelines_compliance.missing_standard_traits (current: 80%, 1.0 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: LruTracker should implement additional standard traits where applicable
    Measurement spec: Evaluates adherence to Rust API guidelines, proper ownership patterns, lifetime management, and zero-cost abstractions with idiomatic Rust patterns
    Evidence found: CacheEntry<V> implements Debug, Clone but LruTracker<K> only implements Debug
    ⚠ This is a PENALTY violation — removing it recovers points

  FIX 22: error_handling_system_coherence.recovery_procedure_completeness (current: 75%, 0.8 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Implements comprehensive recovery procedures with checkpoints, generation consistency validation, and timeout bounds for coordinated subsystem recovery.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if recovery procedures restore all subsystems to consistent state with defined time bounds, 0.7 if comprehensive recovery with some time uncertainty, 0.4 if basic recovery procedures, 0.0 if no coordinated recovery across subsystems
    → ACTION: Add recovery procedure testing and validation to ensure the rollback mechanisms actually restore consistent state under various failure scenarios.
